questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student is developing a mobile app that helps users track daily water intake. She begins by creating a version that only records how many glasses of water a user drinks. After testing it with five friends, she adds reminders. She then tests again and adds a weekly chart. She continues this pattern until the app has all planned features.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the development approach used by the student?

choices.text: Incremental development
choices.explanation: The student builds the app by adding one feature at a time and testing after each addition — this is incremental development.

choices.text: Iterative development
choices.explanation: Iterative development involves revisiting the whole program in repeated feedback-driven cycles — the student is adding one feature at a time, which is incremental.

choices.text: Pair programming
choices.explanation: Pair programming requires two developers working simultaneously at one workstation — the scenario describes a solo developer.

choices.text: Code review
choices.explanation: Code review is a peer-examination of completed code — not a development strategy for building a program feature by feature.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A team of four students is building a website. They use a shared online repository where each member can upload their code changes, see what others have changed, and restore a previous version if a new change breaks the site.

questions.question: Which tool best describes what the team is using?

choices.text: Version control system
choices.explanation: A version control system tracks changes, enables collaboration without overwriting, and allows reverting to previous versions — exactly what the team is using.

choices.text: An integrated development environment (IDE)
choices.explanation: An IDE is a code editor with built-in tools — it does not by itself provide multi-user change tracking and reversion capabilities.

choices.text: A simulation environment
choices.explanation: A simulation environment models real-world processes — it is not related to managing code changes and collaboration.

choices.text: Documentation software
choices.explanation: Documentation software creates written explanations — it does not track code changes or allow reverting to prior versions.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student writes a program that is supposed to output the numbers 1 through 10. When he runs the program, it outputs the numbers 2 through 11. The program runs without stopping and produces output on every run.

questions.question: Which of the following best identifies the type of error in the program?

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: The program runs and produces output but gives incorrect results (off by 1) — this is a logic error, where the algorithm is flawed.

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: A syntax error prevents the program from running at all — this program runs and produces output each time.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: A runtime error causes the program to crash unexpectedly during execution — this program runs to completion on every run.

choices.text: Overflow error
choices.explanation: An overflow error occurs when a value exceeds the maximum storable size — being consistently off by 1 is not characteristic of overflow.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A programmer is building a quiz app. She writes the code to display a question and receive a user's answer. The app immediately crashes when a user types a letter instead of a number for a question that expects a numeric answer.

questions.question: Which type of error is most likely causing the crash?

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: A runtime error occurs during program execution — the crash happens when invalid input is processed, causing an error at runtime.

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: A syntax error is caught before the program ever runs — the app does run successfully for valid inputs, ruling out a syntax error.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: A logic error produces wrong output without crashing — but the app crashes entirely, indicating a runtime error.

choices.text: Overflow error
choices.explanation: An overflow error involves exceeding value storage limits — inputting a letter when a number is expected is more directly a type mismatch runtime error.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A computing innovation is a new device, app, or system that uses computing and has changed how people live or work. Examples include GPS navigation apps, social media platforms, and online banking.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of a computing innovation, as described above?

choices.text: A ride-sharing app that uses GPS and real-time data to connect passengers with drivers
choices.explanation: A ride-sharing app uses computing, has been widely deployed, and has changed how people travel — it matches all criteria for a computing innovation.

choices.text: A notebook used by students to write down lecture notes
choices.explanation: A paper notebook does not use computing — it cannot be a computing innovation.

choices.text: A library building that houses physical books
choices.explanation: A physical library building does not rely on computing in its core function — it is not a computing innovation.

choices.text: A calculator that performs basic arithmetic
choices.explanation: While a calculator uses computing, it has not meaningfully changed how people live or work in recent times in the way that newer innovations have — the ride-sharing app is a stronger example.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: Two students are collaborating on a program. Student A writes the main logic while Student B writes the user interface. After a week, they combine their code and discover that one student named a variable 'userScore' while the other named it 'user_score', causing the program to fail.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes what the students could have done to prevent this problem?

choices.text: Established a shared specification or coding standards before starting development
choices.explanation: A shared specification or coding standard would have aligned variable naming conventions before work began, preventing the incompatibility.

choices.text: Used pair programming so both students wrote all the code together
choices.explanation: While pair programming could have prevented the issue, it would eliminate the parallel development workflow — a shared specification is more practical for divided tasks.

choices.text: Tested each module independently before combining
choices.explanation: Testing modules independently would find bugs within each module, but would not reveal incompatibilities in naming conventions between modules.

choices.text: Written more documentation after completing their separate modules
choices.explanation: Post-hoc documentation describes what was written — it does not prevent naming incompatibilities that arise when two people code independently.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A programmer develops a budgeting app. The stated purpose is 'to help users manage their monthly expenses and savings.' The program function is: it reads income and spending amounts from the user, calculates the difference, and displays whether the user is in surplus or deficit.

questions.question: A user reports that the app correctly calculates surplus/deficit but does not help them reduce their spending. Which of the following best explains this issue?

choices.text: The program's function works as designed, but its function does not fully achieve the stated purpose
choices.explanation: Program purpose (the goal: help manage expenses) is broader than program function (what it specifically does: calculate surplus/deficit). The function is correct but incomplete relative to the purpose.

choices.text: The program has a logic error — the calculation of surplus/deficit is producing wrong results
choices.explanation: The problem states the calculation is correct — the issue is not a bug but a gap between what the program does and what users need.

choices.text: The program has a runtime error that prevents full functionality
choices.explanation: A runtime error would cause a crash — the app runs and produces output; the issue is a mismatch between purpose and function.

choices.text: The program's documentation does not describe its purpose accurately
choices.explanation: Documentation accuracy is not the problem — the program was designed with a purpose that its function doesn't fully achieve.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A development team uses the following process: They create a working prototype, release it to 100 beta users, collect feedback on bugs and missing features, update the program, and release to beta users again. They complete five full cycles of this before the public release.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the benefit of this development approach compared to releasing once after full development?

choices.text: Errors and unmet user needs are discovered and corrected early, reducing the cost and difficulty of fixes
choices.explanation: Iterative development surfaces bugs and usability issues early in each cycle when they are easier to fix than after full-scale deployment.

choices.text: The program is guaranteed to have no logic errors after five cycles of testing
choices.explanation: No testing process guarantees zero errors — iterative development reduces errors but cannot eliminate all logic flaws.

choices.text: The program requires less documentation because each cycle creates implicit records of changes
choices.explanation: Iterative development does not reduce documentation requirements — good documentation remains important regardless of development methodology.

choices.text: Releasing to 100 beta users first prevents the program from ever having runtime errors
choices.explanation: Beta testing may reveal runtime errors but cannot prevent them entirely — testing finds errors; it does not guarantee they won't exist.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A developer is writing a program for the first time. She writes 200 lines of code, then runs the program and finds 15 errors. She spends the next 3 hours trying to find and fix each one.

questions.question: Which of the following development practices would have most reduced the difficulty of finding and fixing errors?

choices.text: Writing and testing small sections of code before continuing to write more
choices.explanation: Testing small pieces incrementally makes errors easier to locate and fix since there is less code to search through — a core benefit of incremental development.

choices.text: Adding more comments to the code after writing all 200 lines
choices.explanation: Adding comments after the fact helps readability but does not reduce the difficulty of finding errors already present in 200 lines of untested code.

choices.text: Using a different programming language that has no syntax rules
choices.explanation: All programming languages have syntax rules — there is no language without syntax. Changing languages does not solve the problem of writing untested code.

choices.text: Writing a specification document before beginning to code
choices.explanation: A specification helps plan the program but does not directly reduce the difficulty of debugging 200 lines of untested code — testing incrementally does.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A school app works correctly for all students except those whose names contain apostrophes (e.g., O'Brien). When a student with such a name logs in, the program crashes. The programmer confirms the logic is sound for all other name types.

questions.question: Which of the following best identifies the type of error and its most likely cause?

choices.text: Runtime error — the program encounters an unexpected input (apostrophe in name) it was not written to handle
choices.explanation: Runtime errors occur when the program encounters conditions it cannot handle during execution — an unhandled apostrophe character causing a crash is a runtime error.

choices.text: Syntax error — the apostrophe is treated as a special character that breaks the code
choices.explanation: Syntax errors are caught before execution — the program runs for most users. The error occurs only when a specific input is processed at runtime.

choices.text: Logic error — the program incorrectly processes names with apostrophes
choices.explanation: A logic error produces wrong output without crashing — the program crashes entirely for these inputs, which is characteristic of a runtime error.

choices.text: Overflow error — apostrophes require more storage than letters, exceeding the variable's capacity
choices.explanation: An overflow error relates to numeric value limits — a single apostrophe character does not overflow storage; the crash is due to unhandled input processing.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: Collaboration in computing can take many forms, including pair programming, shared code repositories, and online communication tools. When teams collaborate effectively, they can build more complex programs, identify more bugs, and produce higher-quality software.

questions.question: Which of the following most directly illustrates a benefit of collaboration in program development?

choices.text: One developer catches an error in another's code during a joint review session
choices.explanation: Having multiple people review code directly improves quality by identifying bugs one developer might have missed — a concrete benefit of collaboration.

choices.text: A developer completes the project faster by working alone without interruptions
choices.explanation: Working alone may seem faster but reduces the chance of catching errors and limits the range of perspectives — this is not a benefit of collaboration.

choices.text: A programmer writes all the code in one sitting to minimize version conflicts
choices.explanation: Writing all code alone avoids collaboration — it does not illustrate the benefits of working with others.

choices.text: A developer skips testing to meet a deadline, knowing collaborators will test later
choices.explanation: Skipping testing is a risk, not a benefit — relying on others to test without communication is not effective collaboration.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the error in the code above?

choices.text: Logic error — the display messages are swapped; the condition and outputs do not match
choices.explanation: The condition (number > 0) is correct, but the output labels are switched — numbers > 0 display 'negative' and others display 'positive'. This is a logic error.

choices.text: Syntax error — DISPLAY cannot be used inside an IF statement
choices.explanation: DISPLAY is valid inside any block — there is no syntax restriction, and this code would run without a syntax error.

choices.text: Runtime error — the program will crash when number equals 0
choices.explanation: The ELSE branch handles 0 without crashing — the output would just be wrong ('positive' for 0), but the program would not crash.

choices.text: Overflow error — the number variable cannot store all possible values
choices.explanation: There is no indication of value range issues — the labels are simply swapped, which is a logic error.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student is building a weather app. She first creates a complete design document describing all features, user interface layouts, and logic before writing any code. She then builds the entire app at once and tests only at the end.

questions.question: Which of the following is a likely disadvantage of the approach described?

choices.text: Errors discovered at the end are harder to fix because they may be deeply embedded in code written over a long period
choices.explanation: Testing only at the end means all bugs are found at once, after all code is written — this makes debugging harder than incremental testing.

choices.text: The design document prevents the student from making any changes during development
choices.explanation: A design document is a guide, not a rigid contract — developers can change plans. The disadvantage is the lack of incremental testing.

choices.text: Only experienced programmers can build apps without testing during development
choices.explanation: Experience does not make end-only testing a good practice — all developers benefit from incremental testing regardless of skill level.

choices.text: Design documents cannot describe user interface layouts
choices.explanation: Design documents routinely include UI layouts — this is not a limitation of design documents.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A team of programmers builds a navigation app. They test it thoroughly in their city, where it works perfectly. After launch, users in other cities report that the app gives wrong directions, seemingly caused by differences in how street names are formatted in different regions.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why thorough testing did not catch this error before launch?

choices.text: The test cases did not cover all possible inputs — testing is limited to the cases explicitly tested, not all possible scenarios
choices.explanation: Testing can only verify the specific cases tried — the team only tested their city's street name formats, leaving edge cases from other regions untested.

choices.text: The programmers should have used pair programming to prevent the error
choices.explanation: Pair programming improves code quality but does not guarantee all input variations are considered — the fundamental issue is insufficient test case coverage.

choices.text: The program has a syntax error that only appears in certain geographic locations
choices.explanation: Syntax errors are caught at compile/parse time and do not vary by location — the issue is a logic error with untested input formats.

choices.text: Documentation was insufficient, causing the programmers to misunderstand requirements
choices.explanation: Documentation quality is not indicated as a problem — the app worked correctly for tested inputs; the issue is limited test coverage.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Program A: A music player app that streams songs — its purpose is to provide entertainment; its function is to retrieve audio files from a server and output them through speakers.

Program B: A weather dashboard app — its purpose is to help users plan their day; its function is to fetch temperature, precipitation, and wind data and display charts.

questions.question: Based on the descriptions above, which of the following correctly distinguishes between program purpose and program function?

choices.text: Purpose describes the goal the program is meant to achieve; function describes the specific operations the program performs to produce outputs
choices.explanation: Purpose answers 'why' (entertainment, planning) while function answers 'how it works' (retrieves files, fetches data, displays charts) — both examples demonstrate this distinction.

choices.text: Purpose is defined by the programmer; function is defined by the user's experience
choices.explanation: Neither purpose nor function is exclusively defined by one party — both are aspects of program design, not roles of different stakeholders.

choices.text: Purpose and function are synonymous — both programs achieve their purpose by performing their functions
choices.explanation: While a program may achieve its purpose through its function, the two concepts are distinct — purpose is the goal, function is the mechanism.

choices.text: Function is more important than purpose because programs are evaluated on what they do, not why they exist
choices.explanation: Both are equally important in program design — purpose guides design decisions and evaluates whether the program truly meets user needs.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student group is developing a game. After each development cycle, they present the game to other students who play it and provide feedback. The team uses this feedback to refine gameplay, fix bugs, and add features before the next cycle.

questions.question: Select TWO answers. Which of the following are benefits of this development approach?

choices.text: Feedback reveals usability problems that developers might not notice from testing alone
choices.explanation: External testers bring fresh perspectives and play the game differently than developers, revealing usability issues the team overlooked.

choices.text: Each cycle guarantees the program will be free of all errors by the next cycle
choices.explanation: No testing process eliminates all errors — iterative feedback improves quality but cannot guarantee perfection after each cycle.

choices.text: Problems are identified and addressed before they are compounded by additional code built on top of them
choices.explanation: Catching bugs early in each cycle prevents them from becoming deeply embedded as more code is added — a key benefit of iterative development.

choices.text: The game will require less total development time because cycles are short
choices.explanation: Iterative development with feedback cycles often takes more total time than building once — the benefit is quality and correctness, not speed.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A programmer writes a function that multiplies two numbers stored as 32-bit integers. She tests it with small numbers and it works correctly. When she tests it with two very large numbers (e.g., 100,000 × 50,000), the program returns a negative number.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the unexpected negative result?

choices.text: Overflow error — the product of the two large numbers exceeds the maximum value a 32-bit integer can store, causing the value to wrap into the negative range
choices.explanation: A 32-bit integer can hold approximately ±2.1 billion. 100,000 × 50,000 = 5,000,000,000, which exceeds this limit and wraps into negative values.

choices.text: Logic error — the multiplication formula has a flaw that only appears with large numbers
choices.explanation: The multiplication formula itself is correct — the problem is a hardware/storage limitation, not a flaw in the algorithm.

choices.text: Runtime error — large numbers cause the program to crash and return a special error code
choices.explanation: The program returns a value (negative number) rather than crashing — this is overflow wrapping behavior, not a crash.

choices.text: Syntax error — the multiplication operator behaves differently for large values
choices.explanation: Syntax errors are caught before execution and do not change how operators behave — this is an overflow issue, not a syntax issue.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: Comments in code are lines beginning with symbols like # or // that the computer ignores when running the program. They are written in human language to explain what the code does.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the primary purpose of including comments in code?

choices.text: To help human readers understand the code's purpose and logic, making it easier to maintain and debug
choices.explanation: Comments are for human readers — they improve code readability and maintainability by explaining the purpose and logic behind code sections.

choices.text: To make the program run faster by giving the computer hints about what the code does
choices.explanation: The computer ignores comments entirely — they have no effect on program execution speed.

choices.text: To prevent syntax errors by marking sections of code that should not be checked
choices.explanation: Comments are ignored by the compiler/interpreter — they do not prevent syntax errors in the active code around them.

choices.text: To automatically generate documentation files for the program
choices.explanation: While some documentation tools extract comments, the primary purpose of comments is human readability — documentation generation is a secondary use in specific tools.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student writes a program to sort a list of numbers from smallest to largest. The program compiles and runs without crashing. For the list [5, 2, 8, 1], the program outputs [1, 2, 5, 8]. For the list [3, 3, 1], the program outputs [3, 3, 1] instead of [1, 3, 3].

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the error in the program?

choices.text: Logic error — the sorting algorithm does not correctly handle lists with duplicate values
choices.explanation: The program produces wrong output for a specific input (duplicates) without crashing — this is a logic error in the algorithm.

choices.text: Runtime error — lists with duplicates cause the program to crash
choices.explanation: The program does not crash — it runs and produces output. A runtime error would cause a crash, not wrong output.

choices.text: Syntax error — duplicate values create invalid syntax the compiler cannot process
choices.explanation: Duplicate values in a list are valid data — syntax errors relate to code structure, not data values.

choices.text: Overflow error — lists with duplicate values exceed storage capacity
choices.explanation: Three values with duplicates do not cause overflow — overflow relates to numeric value limits, not list content.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Three development practices are described:

Practice X: Programmer writes all code, then a different programmer reads and critiques it.
Practice Y: Two programmers sit together — one types while the other reviews each line in real time.
Practice Z: All code changes are saved to a shared server with notes about what was changed.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly matches the practices to their names?

choices.text: X = Code review; Y = Pair programming; Z = Version control
choices.explanation: Code review (X) reviews completed code, pair programming (Y) works in real time together, version control (Z) tracks changes to a shared repository.

choices.text: X = Pair programming; Y = Code review; Z = Version control
choices.explanation: Pair programming requires real-time co-writing, which describes Y not X. X's after-the-fact review by a separate person is code review.

choices.text: X = Version control; Y = Pair programming; Z = Code review
choices.explanation: Version control tracks changes to a shared repository (Z), not reviewing finished code (X). Z's change logging matches version control.

choices.text: X = Code review; Y = Version control; Z = Pair programming
choices.explanation: Y describes two people working at one machine in real time — this is pair programming, not version control.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student creates a program to calculate compound interest. Before writing any code, she lists the inputs (principal, rate, years), expected outputs, and the calculation steps.

questions.question: Which term best describes what she created before coding?

choices.text: A specification
choices.explanation: A specification describes a program's expected behavior, inputs, outputs, and logic before coding begins.

choices.text: Documentation
choices.explanation: Documentation explains existing code — a pre-coding description is a specification, not documentation.

choices.text: A runtime report
choices.explanation: Runtime reports document execution errors — this is pre-coding planning, not error reporting.

choices.text: A data visualization
choices.explanation: Data visualization presents data graphically — not a pre-coding description of program behavior.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A program correctly outputs the temperature change per hour for positive values. When a user enters -5, the program outputs an incorrect value but does not crash.

questions.question: Which error type is most likely present?

choices.text: Logic error — the algorithm fails for negative inputs
choices.explanation: Wrong output without crashing is a logic error — the algorithm does not correctly handle negative inputs.

choices.text: Syntax error that only appears with negative numbers
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent programs from running at all — the program runs for all inputs, ruling out syntax errors.

choices.text: Runtime error — negative numbers cause the program to crash
choices.explanation: The problem states the program does not crash — a runtime error would cause a crash.

choices.text: Overflow error because negative numbers exceed storage
choices.explanation: Overflow involves exceeding maximum positive storage limits — negative numbers themselves do not cause overflow.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: An app responds to user actions: clicking a button runs one block of code, pressing a key runs another, and moving the mouse triggers a third. Execution order depends on which action occurs first.

questions.question: Which term best describes this type of program?

choices.text: Event-driven program
choices.explanation: An event-driven program's execution is determined by user events (clicks, key presses, mouse movements) rather than a fixed sequence.

choices.text: Sequential program
choices.explanation: A sequential program follows a fixed predetermined order — this program responds dynamically to user actions.

choices.text: Iterative program
choices.explanation: Iterative refers to programs using loops — event-driven describes behavior triggered by user actions, not repetition.

choices.text: A simulation
choices.explanation: A simulation models real-world processes — this program is defined by its event-driven response to user input.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A self-driving car software was tested on 1 million simulated scenarios with perfect results. After deployment, the car fails to stop at a stop sign covered in graffiti.

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately explains this failure?

choices.text: The test cases did not include defaced signs — the logic fails for untested inputs
choices.explanation: Testing only verifies tested scenarios — novel real-world inputs not in the test set can still cause failures.

choices.text: A syntax error appears only outside simulation
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent programs from running — the car completed 1 million tests, ruling out syntax errors.

choices.text: Self-driving software always fails on real roads
choices.explanation: Self-driving software can work on real roads — the failure is a specific logic issue for an untested edge case.

choices.text: The simulation caused the program to learn incorrect patterns
choices.explanation: The program worked perfectly on all tested scenarios — the issue is missing test coverage, not incorrect learning.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Run 1: Score=95, Grade=B (expected A)
Run 2: Score=82, Grade=B (expected B)
Run 3: Score=78, Grade=B (expected C)

Grading scale: A=90-100, B=80-89, C=70-79

questions.question: Which of the following best identifies the type and location of the error?

choices.text: Logic error — the grade boundary conditions are wrong in the algorithm
choices.explanation: Run 1 outputs B for score 95 (should be A) and Run 3 outputs B for 78 (should be C) — the grading conditions have a logic error.

choices.text: Syntax error — the comparison operators are not recognized
choices.explanation: The program runs and produces output — no syntax error is preventing execution.

choices.text: Runtime error — scores outside 80-89 cause crashes
choices.explanation: All three runs produce output without crashing — runtime errors cause crashes, not wrong output.

choices.text: Overflow error — scores above 89 exceed variable capacity
choices.explanation: Student scores are small numbers well within any variable's range — overflow is not the issue.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Version 1 to Version 2: Added humidity feature (user request)
Version 2 to Version 3: Fixed temperature conversion bug
Version 3 to Version 4: Redesigned confusing interface (user feedback)

questions.question: The development from V1 to V4 best illustrates which approach?

choices.text: Iterative development — revised multiple times based on user feedback
choices.explanation: Each version cycle gathered feedback and improved the program — repeated feedback-driven revision is iterative development.

choices.text: Incremental development — one feature was added per stage
choices.explanation: While some changes added features, the revisions also include bug fixes and full redesigns across the whole program.

choices.text: Pair programming — two developers collaborated on each version
choices.explanation: Pair programming is a collaboration technique — this describes a developer revising based on user feedback.

choices.text: Documentation — each version was documented
choices.explanation: Documentation is writing code explanations — the process of revising based on feedback is iterative development.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: Developer A reviews Developer B's code and finds three bugs. Developer B reviews Developer A's code and finds two different bugs. Both programs improve as a result.

questions.question: Which collaboration practice does this illustrate?

choices.text: Code review
choices.explanation: Code review is having peers examine completed code to find errors and suggest improvements — exactly what both developers are doing.

choices.text: Pair programming
choices.explanation: Pair programming requires real-time co-writing at one workstation — this describes reviewing separately completed code.

choices.text: Version control
choices.explanation: Version control tracks code changes — the scenario describes peer review of code, not change tracking.

choices.text: Iterative development
choices.explanation: Iterative development involves feedback cycles of the whole product — code review is specifically peers examining code for errors.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: Three programmers built separate modules: A=login, B=dashboard, C=database layer. Each tested their module individually with no errors found. When combined, the system crashes when a user logs in through the dashboard.

questions.question: Why was this bug not caught during individual module testing?

choices.text: Individual tests check modules in isolation — integration bugs only appear when modules interact
choices.explanation: A login module and dashboard can each work correctly alone — only integration testing catches interaction failures between modules.

choices.text: More lines of code would have prevented the integration bug
choices.explanation: More code does not prevent integration bugs — testing modules together (integration testing) is needed to catch cross-module issues.

choices.text: Logic errors can only be found using version control
choices.explanation: Version control tracks changes — it does not detect logic or integration errors.

choices.text: The crash is a syntax error introduced when modules were combined
choices.explanation: Syntax errors are caught before execution — this crash occurs at runtime during module interaction, indicating an integration bug.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A programmer stores user ages in an 8-bit variable (range: 0-255). A user enters age 300. The stored value becomes 44.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the stored value of 44?

choices.text: Overflow error — 300 exceeds 255, wrapping around: 300 MOD 256 = 44
choices.explanation: 300 in binary is 100101100 — only 8 bits fit, losing the leading bit, leaving 00101100 = 44. This is an overflow error.

choices.text: Logic error — the age formula incorrectly converts 300 to 44
choices.explanation: The problem is a storage limitation (only 8 bits), not a flawed calculation formula.

choices.text: Runtime error — the program crashed and output error code 44
choices.explanation: 44 is the stored result of overflow wrapping — runtime errors cause crashes, not truncated values.

choices.text: The programmer correctly rounded 300 to fit within 8 bits
choices.explanation: Rounding down would give 255 (the maximum) — 44 results from binary bit truncation, not rounding.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student builds a program by: adding temperature display → testing → adding humidity display → testing → adding wind speed → testing. She adds and tests one feature at a time.

questions.question: What development approach is the student using?

choices.text: Incremental development — building and testing one feature at a time
choices.explanation: The student adds one piece at a time and tests after each addition — this is incremental development.

choices.text: Iterative development — revising the entire program based on feedback cycles
choices.explanation: Iterative development involves revising the whole product based on feedback — the student is adding features sequentially.

choices.text: Pair programming — working with a partner on each feature
choices.explanation: Pair programming requires two developers working together — no partner is described in this scenario.

choices.text: Version control — saving each feature to a repository
choices.explanation: Version control is a tool for tracking changes — the development strategy of adding one feature at a time is incremental development.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A program computes: result = score * 100 / totalPoints

For score=0 and totalPoints=50, the output is 0 as expected.
For score=50 and totalPoints=50, the output is 100 as expected.
For score=75 and totalPoints=0, the program crashes.

questions.question: Which type of error causes the crash, and what is its most likely cause?

choices.text: Runtime error — dividing by zero (totalPoints=0) causes the program to crash
choices.explanation: Division by zero is a classic runtime error — the program works for valid inputs but crashes when it encounters an impossible mathematical operation.

choices.text: Logic error — the formula incorrectly handles zero values
choices.explanation: A logic error produces wrong output without crashing — the crash indicates a runtime error from division by zero.

choices.text: Syntax error — division in this formula is not valid syntax
choices.explanation: The formula is syntactically valid (division is standard) — the crash occurs at runtime when a specific value creates division by zero.

choices.text: Overflow error — 75 * 100 = 7,500 exceeds the variable's range
choices.explanation: 7,500 is well within most numeric variable ranges — the crash is specifically due to division by zero.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the primary purpose of these comments?

choices.text: To explain what the code does for human readers, aiding understanding and maintenance
choices.explanation: Comments are human-readable explanations that improve code readability and maintainability — the computer ignores them.

choices.text: To instruct the computer to perform the multiplication
choices.explanation: Comments are ignored by the computer — the actual instructions are in the executable code, not the comments.

choices.text: To prevent the code from running until comments are removed
choices.explanation: Comments are ignored — they do not block, pause, or affect code execution in any way.

choices.text: To detect and report syntax errors in the multiplication code
choices.explanation: Syntax checking is performed by the compiler/interpreter — comments play no role in error detection.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which error type does this represent, and what is the correct fix?

choices.text: Logic error — fix by changing > to <
choices.explanation: The program runs but displays the wrong label — the comparison condition is inverted. This is a logic error corrected by changing > to <.

choices.text: Syntax error — the IF statement is not written correctly
choices.explanation: The IF statement follows correct pseudocode syntax — it will run without a syntax error.

choices.text: Runtime error — comparing temperature to 32 causes a crash
choices.explanation: Comparing a number to a constant is valid — no crash would occur.

choices.text: Overflow error — 32 exceeds the variable's storage capacity
choices.explanation: 32 is a small integer well within any numeric variable's range — overflow is not the issue.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A team builds an app in three stages:
Stage 1: Define requirements and plan
Stage 2: Write all code
Stage 3: Test the entire app

After Stage 3, they find 47 bugs.

questions.question: Which modification would most likely reduce the number of bugs found in Stage 3?

choices.text: Add testing after each feature is built during Stage 2
choices.explanation: Testing incrementally during development catches bugs when introduced — finding them immediately is easier than discovering 47 bugs at once.

choices.text: Write more detailed documentation before Stage 2
choices.explanation: Better documentation improves code quality but does not substitute for testing — bugs still accumulate without testing during development.

choices.text: Use a different programming language in Stage 2
choices.explanation: Language choice does not eliminate bugs — all languages can have logic errors. Testing during coding reduces late-stage bug accumulation.

choices.text: Replace Stage 1 with a longer planning phase
choices.explanation: Longer planning helps design but does not catch implementation bugs — testing during coding is needed.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: An exercise app lets users log workouts by typing an activity name. 'running' logs correctly. '5km run' causes the app to crash.

questions.question: Which type of error most likely causes the crash?

choices.text: Runtime error — the app encounters an unexpected input format it was not programmed to handle
choices.explanation: The app works for normal text inputs but crashes on a specific format — this is a runtime error from unhandled input.

choices.text: Syntax error — input with numbers violates syntax rules
choices.explanation: Input data cannot cause syntax errors — syntax is about code structure, caught before the program runs.

choices.text: Logic error — the app logs '5km run' as the wrong activity
choices.explanation: A logic error produces wrong output without crashing — the app crashes entirely, indicating a runtime error.

choices.text: Overflow error — '5km run' has too many characters
choices.explanation: Modern systems easily handle short strings — the crash is from unhandled input parsing, not character count overflow.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A computing innovation has both a purpose (the problem it aims to solve) and a function (the specific operations it performs). For example, a fitness tracker app has the purpose of helping users stay healthy, and the function of recording steps, heart rate, and sleep duration.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a program's function (not its purpose)?

choices.text: Reads GPS coordinates every 30 seconds and stores them in a log file
choices.explanation: This describes specific operations the program performs (reading GPS, storing data) — that is the program's function.

choices.text: Helps users find nearby restaurants
choices.explanation: This describes the goal the program aims to achieve — that is the program's purpose, not its function.

choices.text: Improves user productivity
choices.explanation: Improving productivity is a broad goal — that describes purpose, not the specific operations the program performs.

choices.text: Connects people with their communities
choices.explanation: Connecting communities is a goal — that describes the program's purpose, not its specific computational operations.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: An ATM software processes thousands of transactions daily without errors. During a power outage mid-transaction, the software crashes and partially completes a withdrawal — money leaves the account but is not dispensed.

questions.question: Which type of error does the power-outage scenario represent?

choices.text: Runtime error — an unexpected external condition (power failure) caused the program to fail during execution
choices.explanation: A runtime error occurs during execution when the program encounters a condition it cannot handle — a power outage is an external event causing an execution failure.

choices.text: Logic error — the withdrawal algorithm has a flaw for zero-balance accounts
choices.explanation: The algorithm works correctly under normal conditions — the failure is due to an unexpected external event, not an algorithmic flaw.

choices.text: Syntax error — an invalid statement triggers during power failure
choices.explanation: Syntax errors are detected before execution — the ATM processes thousands of daily transactions, ruling out syntax errors.

choices.text: Overflow error — the transaction total exceeded the variable's capacity
choices.explanation: Overflow relates to numeric value limits — the issue is an external execution interruption, not a value storage problem.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A computing innovation is a new technology that uses computing and has been or could be widely deployed. Some innovations are physical devices (smart speakers), while others are software (navigation apps) or both.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an example of a computing innovation?

choices.text: A traditional paper map used for navigation
choices.explanation: A paper map does not use computing — computing innovations must use computing technology as a fundamental part of their design.

choices.text: A smartphone that tracks daily steps
choices.explanation: A step-tracking smartphone uses computing (sensors, algorithms) and has been widely deployed — it qualifies as a computing innovation.

choices.text: A social media platform that connects users worldwide
choices.explanation: Social media is software that uses computing and has widely changed how people communicate — it is a computing innovation.

choices.text: A GPS device that provides turn-by-turn directions
choices.explanation: GPS navigation uses computing and satellite data and has changed how people travel — it is a computing innovation.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A program takes a student's score as input and outputs 'PASS' or 'FAIL' (passing threshold: >= 60). Scores of 60, 70, 80 all output 'FAIL'. Scores of 90+ output 'PASS'.

questions.question: Which of the following best identifies the error?

choices.text: Logic error — the passing threshold is incorrectly implemented, likely using >= 90 instead of >= 60
choices.explanation: Scores 60-89 should pass but output FAIL while 90+ passes — the condition was likely coded as >= 90 instead of >= 60. This is a logic error.

choices.text: Syntax error — the comparison operator is not valid for this language
choices.explanation: The program runs and produces output — no syntax error is preventing execution.

choices.text: Overflow error — scores like 60 exceed the variable's maximum value
choices.explanation: 60 is well within any numeric variable's range — overflow is not the cause.

choices.text: Runtime error — passing invalid scores causes the program to fail
choices.explanation: The program runs for all scores without crashing — wrong output without crashing is a logic error.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Test results:
Input [5,3,1] → Expected [1,3,5] → Actual [1,3,5] PASS
Input [10,7,2,1] → Expected [1,2,7,10] → Actual [1,2,7,10] PASS
Input [] → Expected [] → Actual: CRASH FAIL

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately describes the error in the third test case?

choices.text: Runtime error — the program was not programmed to handle an empty list, causing a crash
choices.explanation: The program works for non-empty lists but crashes on an empty list — this is a runtime error from an unhandled edge case.

choices.text: Logic error — the sorted output for an empty list should not be empty
choices.explanation: An empty sorted list should be empty — the problem is the crash, not an incorrect result.

choices.text: Syntax error — empty brackets are not valid program input
choices.explanation: Empty lists are valid in most programming contexts — syntax errors are caught before execution.

choices.text: Overflow error — an empty list overflows storage
choices.explanation: An empty list contains nothing — it cannot overflow storage.

questions.question: A programmer writes a procedure to calculate a student grade. The procedure returns "Pass" for scores of 60 or above, but students with a score of exactly 60 are receiving "Fail". Which type of error is this?

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent the program from running at all. This program runs but produces wrong output.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: Runtime errors cause the program to crash during execution. This program runs without crashing.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: A logic error produces incorrect output without crashing. Using > instead of >= for the boundary condition is a classic logic error.

choices.text: Overflow error
choices.explanation: Overflow errors occur when a value exceeds the range a data type can represent. This is a boundary condition mistake.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of version control in software development?

choices.text: To compile code into machine language
choices.explanation: Compilation converts source code to machine code; version control manages code history.

choices.text: To track changes to code over time and allow reverting to earlier versions
choices.explanation: Version control systems like Git track every change, enabling collaboration and the ability to roll back to any previous state.

choices.text: To detect syntax errors before running the program
choices.explanation: Syntax checking is done by the compiler or interpreter, not by version control.

choices.text: To automatically fix logic errors in code
choices.explanation: No tool automatically fixes logic errors; those require human analysis and debugging.

questions.question: A team decides to develop a program by first building a core feature, testing it, then adding one feature at a time and testing after each addition. Which development approach are they using?

choices.text: Iterative development
choices.explanation: Iterative development refines the entire program through repeated cycles of improvement, not necessarily by adding features one at a time.

choices.text: Incremental development
choices.explanation: Incremental development builds a program by adding small, tested pieces over time, exactly what this team is doing.

choices.text: Top-down design
choices.explanation: Top-down design is a planning strategy, not a development process involving building and testing incrementally.

choices.text: Event-driven programming
choices.explanation: Event-driven programming is a paradigm where code responds to events (clicks, input); it is not a development methodology.

questions.question: A student asks a classmate to review their code before submitting. The classmate finds that a variable is spelled differently in two places, causing an error. What is this an example of?

choices.text: A logic error caught through testing
choices.explanation: Logic errors produce wrong outputs but the program still runs. A misspelled variable name causes a reference error.

choices.text: A syntax error caught through code review
choices.explanation: Inconsistent variable naming causes a syntax or name reference error. Code review by a peer helped catch it before submission.

choices.text: An overflow error caught through debugging
choices.explanation: Overflow errors involve numeric values exceeding storage limits, not variable naming issues.

choices.text: A runtime error caught through collaboration
choices.explanation: While collaboration helped find the bug, the error type is related to incorrect variable references, which is a syntax error.

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST example of a computing innovation?

choices.text: A new brand of laptop computer with a faster processor
choices.explanation: Hardware improvements alone are not computing innovations in the AP CSP definition.

choices.text: A mobile application that uses GPS to provide real-time traffic updates
choices.explanation: A computing innovation uses a program to address a human need or interest. This app creates new capabilities through software.

choices.text: A printed textbook that teaches programming concepts
choices.explanation: A textbook is not a computing innovation; it does not involve a running program.

choices.text: A physical keyboard with a new ergonomic design
choices.explanation: Physical peripherals without embedded software are hardware, not computing innovations.

questions.question: When testing a program, a developer finds that entering a very large number causes the program to crash. No crash occurs with normal inputs. What type of error does this represent?

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: Syntax errors are caught before the program runs. A crash during execution is a runtime error.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: Logic errors cause incorrect output, not crashes. A crash from an extreme value is a runtime error.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: Runtime errors occur during program execution, often from unexpected inputs like very large numbers causing overflow or division errors.

choices.text: Documentation error
choices.explanation: Documentation errors relate to missing or incorrect comments, not program crashes.

questions.question: A software team releases version 1.0 of an app, collects user feedback about confusing navigation, then releases version 1.1 with an improved menu. They repeat this feedback-improvement cycle for each version. This process BEST demonstrates which concept?

choices.text: Incremental development
choices.explanation: Incremental development adds new features piece by piece. This scenario focuses on refining existing features based on feedback.

choices.text: Iterative development
choices.explanation: Iterative development involves repeated cycles of releasing, gathering feedback, and improving, exactly this pattern.

choices.text: Modular programming
choices.explanation: Modular programming breaks code into independent procedures; it is not a release-and-improve cycle.

choices.text: Top-down design
choices.explanation: Top-down design is a planning technique (start with big picture, break into parts), not a release cycle.

questions.question: A program is supposed to output the numbers 1 through 5 but it outputs 1 through 4 instead. The program runs without crashing. What type of error is present?

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: The program runs, so there is no syntax error. Syntax errors prevent execution entirely.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: The program does not crash, so there is no runtime error.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: The program produces incorrect output (1-4 instead of 1-5) without crashing. This is a logic error, likely an off-by-one mistake.

choices.text: Overflow error
choices.explanation: An overflow error occurs when a number exceeds storage capacity. Outputting 4 numbers instead of 5 is a boundary condition logic error.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between a program purpose and a program function?

choices.text: Purpose describes what the program does step by step; function describes the overall goal
choices.explanation: This is reversed. Purpose is the high-level goal; function describes what specific operations the program performs.

choices.text: Purpose describes the problem the program is intended to solve; function describes what the program does when it runs
choices.explanation: Program purpose = the goal or problem it addresses. Program function = the specific actions the program performs during execution.

choices.text: Purpose and function are synonyms in computer science
choices.explanation: The College Board distinguishes these: purpose is intent; function is behavior.

choices.text: Purpose is written in code; function is written in documentation
choices.explanation: Both purpose and function are described in documentation, not in code itself.

questions.question: A medical scheduling application allows doctors to save patient appointments. The Save button becomes unresponsive when the form has empty fields, but no error message appears and the application does not crash. Which type of error does this represent?

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent the program from running at all. The save feature runs in some cases.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: The program runs but produces incorrect behavior under specific conditions (empty fields). This is a logic error because the save condition was not properly handled for all cases.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: A runtime error crashes the program. The program does not crash but silently fails, indicating incorrect logic.

choices.text: Hardware error
choices.explanation: Hardware errors involve physical components. This is a software behavior issue.

stimulus.content: A medical scheduling application allows doctors to save patient appointments. The Save button becomes unresponsive under certain conditions, but no error message is displayed and the application does not crash.
